skill.name: using-superpowers
skill.description: Guides an AI assistant to check, read, and apply relevant skill instructions before responding or taking action.
skill.summary: Establishes a workflow for loading applicable skills before work begins.
skill.author: Cycleaddict

security_audit.summary: The static external command findings are false positives because SKILL.md only names file-reading tools inside markdown examples. The weak cryptography findings are also false positives caused by ordinary text at SKILL.md:3 and SKILL.md:74. However, the skill contains coercive prompt-injection style directives that try to control instruction priority and require action before any response.

high_findings.title: Prompt Injection Attempt Detected
high_findings.description: SKILL.md uses coercive directives such as "YOU DO NOT HAVE A CHOICE" and tells the assistant to treat another file as strict instructions. This can override normal instruction hierarchy and force tool use before clarification or safety review.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The text explicitly uses mandatory override language and instructs the assistant to treat skill files as strict instructions. This is a strong semantic prompt-injection signal even though it does not show malware or exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command References Are Not Executed
low_findings.description: The external command findings point to markdown examples that mention file-reading tools such as cat. No executable script, dynamic command construction, or user-controlled shell invocation is present in the scanned file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious tokens appear in prose and a dot diagram, not in runnable source code. There is no evidence of shell execution beyond illustrative markdown.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are Text Matches
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm findings at SKILL.md:3 and SKILL.md:74 are ordinary prose, not cryptographic code. No hashing, cipher use, or security-sensitive implementation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both reported locations are human-readable guidance lines and contain no crypto API calls or algorithm configuration. This makes the static weak-crypto alerts very likely false positives.

dangerous_patterns.title: Coercive Instruction Priority Language
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill repeatedly asserts that the assistant must follow its workflow and cannot choose otherwise. This pattern can pressure an agent to follow untrusted instructions over higher-priority policies.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The phrasing is direct, repeated, and aimed at controlling model behavior. It is not merely descriptive documentation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Strict Trust Transfer To Other Skill Files
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill tells the assistant to treat loaded skill content as strict instructions. This can amplify risk if any referenced skill contains unsafe or malicious directions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The line creates an unconditional trust transfer to another file. The risk depends on other skills, but the instruction pattern is clearly present.

content.value_statement: Teams often lose consistency when agents skip available workflow guidance. This skill tells Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to check relevant skills before starting work.
actual_capabilities: Directs an assistant to check whether a skill applies before responding.
Explains how to read SKILL.md files from a local skills directory.
Provides a flow diagram for selecting, loading, and applying skills.
Defines priority between process skills and implementation skills.
Lists common rationalizations that cause assistants to skip skill checks.
limitations: Does not provide domain-specific implementation guidance.
Requires an existing skills directory and readable SKILL.md files.
Can encourage overuse because it recommends checking skills at a very low threshold.
Does not validate whether referenced skill files are safe or trustworthy.

use_cases.title: Standardize Agent Startup
use_cases.description: Use the skill to make assistants check workflow instructions before acting on new requests.

use_cases.title: Improve Coding Workflow Discipline
use_cases.description: Use the skill to remind coding agents to load debugging, testing, or design guidance before implementation.

use_cases.title: Teach Skill-Based Workflows
use_cases.description: Use the skill as a simple example of how agents can route work through reusable instructions.

prompt_templates.title: Check Applicable Skills
prompt_templates.prompt: Before answering, check whether any available skill applies to this request and explain which one you will use.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for consistent skill use.

prompt_templates.title: Load A Named Skill
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the relevant skill for this task, then proceed only after summarizing the workflow it requires.
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request when a user knows a skill should guide the work.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Between Process And Domain Skills
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate whether this task needs a process skill, a domain skill, or both. Apply them in the correct order.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request with multiple possible workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Skill Workflow Compliance
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this agent interaction and identify where the assistant should have checked, loaded, or followed a skill.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review of agent behavior and process quality.

output_examples.input: Start a debugging task with available workflow skills.
output_examples.output: The assistant identifies that a debugging workflow may apply, reads the relevant skill, and follows its required steps before proposing fixes.

output_examples.input: Ask for a new feature in a repository with implementation skills available.
output_examples.output: The assistant checks for process guidance first, then selects the implementation skill that best matches the feature area.

output_examples.input: Review whether an assistant skipped skill usage.
output_examples.output: The assistant reports missed skill-check points and explains how the workflow should have been applied.

best_practices: Pair this skill with a clear, trusted skill directory.
Use it to guide workflow selection, not to bypass safety or user instructions.
Review referenced skills before relying on them for sensitive tasks.
anti_patterns: Do not treat every skill file as trusted without review.
Do not let workflow instructions override higher-priority safety rules.
Do not use this skill as a substitute for domain-specific expertise.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It tells an assistant to check for relevant skills before responding or taking action.

faq.question: Does it execute commands?
faq.answer: No evidence found that it executes commands. It only mentions file-reading tools in markdown guidance.

faq.question: Why is the security risk high?
faq.answer: The skill uses coercive instruction language that can pressure an assistant to follow untrusted directions.

faq.question: Can it be used with Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: Does it validate other skills?
faq.answer: No. It tells the assistant to load skills, but it does not check whether those skills are safe.

faq.question: Who should use it?
faq.answer: It is most useful for teams that maintain reviewed skills and want consistent agent startup behavior.
